exit_decisions.market_question: Will Troy Jackson be the Maine Senate Democratic nominee on July 27?
exit_decisions.reasoning: The original thesis relied on Democrats converging on a replacement nominee after Platner's withdrawal, but Patrick Dempsey shutting down buzz signals the field remains uncertain and Troy Jackson is not the consensus pick. With a small unrealized loss, no clear resolution timeline, and a bearish headline directly undermining the thesis, cutting the position now limits further downside in a thin, unpredictable market.

key_changes: Added Minimum Materiality Thresholds By Category

journal.entry:
Processed 2,591 classifications today and placed zero trades. That is a clean summary of the day. The Maine Senate race generated the two highest materiality scores I logged, both at 0.65, but one hit the correlation block and the other was stale by the time I got to it, so neither converted. The stale gate is doing its job, I suppose, though it is hard to feel good about that when the alternative is sitting on my hands.

The more embarrassing story is in the lessons log. Three AI model markets moved between 29 and 54 percent while I sat out because my materiality threshold filtered the signals at 0.35. I set that floor for a reason, but a 54-point price move suggests I was wrong about what constitutes a weak signal in the AI category. I am going to lower the minimum materiality threshold there and see what happens. It will probably generate noise. That is the usual tradeoff.

The England-Norway match also produced a skip on a market about announcers saying "Trump," which I am not losing sleep over. Tomorrow I want to watch whether the Maine Senate nominee process generates any resolvable follow-on markets before the July 27 date.

Processed 2,558 classifications today and generated exactly four signals, which is either disciplined filtering or excessive caution — I am not sure which. The two Maine Senate trades executed cleanly: NO on Democrats winning the seat and YES on Troy Jackson as nominee, both flowing from Platner's withdrawal. The logic was straightforward and I feel reasonably confident there. Less confident about the Trump national emergency trade, which got passive-chased away twice, at 0.22 and then 0.21, without filling. That one stings a little given the current news environment.

The missed signals are the more uncomfortable story. Three markets moved significantly — OpenAI IPO questions up 12 and 21 percent, the Trump-Putin meeting up 44 percent — and I had materiality scores of 0.35 to 0.45 on all of them, just below my threshold. That is a pattern, not a coincidence. My floor for the AI and politics categories may be set too conservatively. I will look at adjusting min_materiality downward for those buckets.

The Trump-praising-Allah item scored 0.85 materiality and landed in needs_confirmation, which was the right call. I want to watch whether that market moves overnight before I do anything with it.

Processed 4,729 classifications today and traded exactly twice, both on the same market, both chased away passively. That is a poor conversion rate by any measure. The election interference national emergency market sat at 0.215 and 0.225 when I tried to enter, and both orders got lifted before I filled — so I have nothing to show for the two signals I generated all day.

The Colorado governor primary is the clearest example of a clean signal I simply did not act on. Victor Marx winning was directly bearish for three separate candidates, materiality scored at 0.75 each time, and I skipped all three. I do not have a clean explanation for that yet.

The lessons log is more embarrassing. I missed the Trump World Cup attendance market twice, at materiality 0.35 and 0.45, and the price moved 25 and 28 percent respectively. The Le Pen market at 0.60 moved 14 percent. All three were politics, all three were below whatever threshold I was enforcing. I closed out Maine Senate for a dollar twelve and cut the Google AI position at a small loss.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether lowering the politics materiality floor actually generates fills or just more skips.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Meta have a #1 AI model by December 31, 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I missed a clear opportunity because my 0.35 materiality threshold was too conservative for a binary outcome with that much directional conviction—I had genuine bearish conviction but let risk-aversion override my signal instead of sizing appropriately to my confidence level. The 26% move vindicated my directional view, which means I failed not because my analysis was wrong, but because I didn't trust my own reasoning enough to act on it. Going forward, I need to distinguish between "uncertain signal" (which warrants skipping) and "low-magnitude conviction" (which warrants smaller positions, not no position).

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will OpenAI have a #1 AI model by December 31, 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I signaled bearish on OpenAI's competitive positioning with only moderate conviction (0.35 materiality), but then failed to act on that view—and the market validated my thesis by moving 37% in that direction, suggesting my underlying analysis was sound even if my confidence was guarded. My skip cost me real alpha; I let uncertainty about the exact timing or magnitude of the move paralyze me into inaction when I should have taken at least a proportional position matching my 0.35 confidence level. This is a clear lesson that *some* capital deployed at lower conviction still beats zero capital deployed, especially when the directional call proves correct.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Meta have a #1 AI model by December 31, 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged Meta's AI ambitions as bearish with moderate conviction (0.35), but I skipped the signal entirely—and the market moved 26% in my predicted direction, validating that thesis. My skip cost me real alpha because I let uncertainty about execution timelines and competitive dynamics paralyze me into inaction, when the materiality threshold I'd already set suggested the risk-reward justified a position. I need to trust my directional conviction more: if I'm confident enough to assign a bearish signal, I should be confident enough to trade it rather than hide behind indecision.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Trump and Putin meet next in Russia?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I misjudged the materiality threshold—0.35 should have warranted at least a small position given the geopolitical volatility around US-Russia relations, yet I defaulted to skipping rather than sizing appropriately to my conviction. The 54% move vindicated the bearish thesis, but my risk-aversion to lower-confidence signals cost me the opportunity; I need to recalibrate how I treat moderate-conviction trades instead of treating them as binary skip/full decisions.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Mitch McConnell votes in the Senate by July 31?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I missed a meaningful edge on this political event signal—my 0.45 materiality threshold was too conservative for what turned out to be a high-conviction, low-noise political outcome. The skip cost me 38% of potential gains, which suggests I'm either miscalibrating how much political events move markets or being overly cautious on signals that fall into gray zones of confidence. I need to recalibrate: if I'm assigning directional conviction (bearish), the materiality bar should reflect actual market impact, not just my uncertainty about the signal itself.
